Floating trains.<break time=800ms/> No wheels needed.<break time=800ms/>

The term electromagnetic train comes from using magnets to lift and propel a train—replacing steel wheels with invisible forces.<break time=800ms/> Most trains fight friction;<break time=200ms/> these erase it.<break time=800ms/>

Here’s how:<break time=100ms/> The track is lined with electromagnets, their poles flipping in waves like a stadium crowd doing "the wave." The train’s own magnets either repel (pushing up to float) or attract (pulling forward).<break time=800ms/> No contact.<break time=800ms/> No wear.<break time=800ms/> A Japanese SCMaglev once hit 375 miles per hour—faster than a Formula 1 car—because air resistance was its only enemy.<break time=800ms/>

But magnets demand energy.<break time=800ms/> Germany’s Transrapid used 30% of its power just to hover.<break time=800ms/> China’s Shanghai Maglev solves this by switching to wheels below 62 miles per hour, saving juice for high-speed bursts.<break time=800ms/>

Three related subjects are superconducting magnets, hyperloop vacuum tubes, and how birds exploit air currents.<break time=800ms/>
clean_story:
Floating trains. No wheels needed.

The term electromagnetic train comes from using magnets to lift and propel a train—replacing steel wheels with invisible forces. Most trains fight friction; these erase it.

Here’s how: The track is lined with electromagnets, their poles flipping in waves like a stadium crowd doing "the wave." The train’s own magnets either repel (pushing up to float) or attract (pulling forward). No contact. No wear. A Japanese SCMaglev once hit 375 miles per hour—faster than a Formula 1 car—because air resistance was its only enemy.

But magnets demand energy. Germany’s Transrapid used 30% of its power just to hover. China’s Shanghai Maglev solves this by switching to wheels below 62 miles per hour, saving juice for high-speed bursts.

Three related subjects are superconducting magnets, hyperloop vacuum tubes, and how birds exploit air currents.
